skill.name: svelte-code-writer
skill.description: CLI tools for Svelte 5 documentation lookup and code analysis. MUST be used whenever creating, editing or analyzing any Svelte component (.svelte) or Svelte module (.svelte.ts/.svelte.js). If possible, this skill should be executed within the svelte-file-editor agent for optimal results.
skill.summary: Official Svelte MCP tools for documentation lookup, code analysis, and automated fixing of Svelte 5 components.
skill.author: sveltejs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 22 external_commands patterns and 1 filesystem pattern, all false positives. The detected patterns are markdown documentation examples showing legitimate npx CLI command usage (@sveltejs/mcp). No actual code execution, network calls, or filesystem operations exist in the skill content. Safe for publication.

content.user_title: Build and fix Svelte 5 components with official tools
content.value_statement: Struggling with Svelte 5 runes and modern patterns? Access official documentation and automated code fixes directly in your workflow.
actual_capabilities: List all available Svelte 5 and SvelteKit documentation sections
Retrieve full documentation for specific topics like $state, $derived, $effect
Analyze Svelte components for common issues and suggest fixes
Support both Svelte 4 and Svelte 5 target versions
Handle async Svelte components with proper configuration
limitations: Requires npx and Node.js environment to execute CLI commands
Autofixer suggestions are advisory and require manual review
Shell escaping required for $ runes when passing inline code

use_cases.title: Learning Svelte 5 Runes
use_cases.description: New to Svelte 5? Look up documentation for $state, $derived, and $effect runes to understand modern reactivity patterns.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developers migrating from Svelte 4 or other frameworks

use_cases.title: Debugging Component Issues
use_cases.description: Run the autofixer on problematic components to detect common mistakes like state mutations in effects.
use_cases.target_user: Svelte developers debugging runtime errors or unexpected behavior

use_cases.title: Code Review Validation
use_cases.description: Validate Svelte components before merging by running the autofixer to catch issues early.
use_cases.target_user: Tech leads and reviewers ensuring code quality in team projects

prompt_templates.title: Basic Documentation Lookup
prompt_templates.prompt: List all available Svelte documentation sections, then retrieve the documentation for $state and $derived runes.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to understand specific Svelte 5 features

prompt_templates.title: Component Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Svelte component for issues: [paste component code or provide file path]
prompt_templates.scenario: When reviewing or debugging an existing component

prompt_templates.title: Svelte 4 Migration Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Check this component for Svelte 5 compatibility using the autofixer with --svelte-version 5 flag.
prompt_templates.scenario: When migrating a Svelte 4 codebase to Svelte 5

prompt_templates.title: Async Component Validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate this async Svelte component with the autofixer, ensuring await usage is properly configured.
prompt_templates.scenario: When working with async Svelte components that use top-level await

output_examples.input: Analyze a component with potential rune issues
output_examples.output: The autofixer detects assignments to $state variables inside $effect blocks and suggests using proper state updates instead.
Returns comprehensive documentation explaining $effect usage, lifecycle timing, cleanup patterns, and common pitfalls.

best_practices: Always run list-sections first to discover available documentation topics before requesting specific docs
Execute svelte-autofixer before finalizing any Svelte component to catch issues early
Escape $ characters as \$ when passing inline code containing runes via terminal commands
anti_patterns: Using the autofixer without reviewing its suggestions - always validate recommendations
Passing large codebases as inline arguments instead of file paths
Forgetting to specify --async flag when analyzing components with top-level await

faq.question: What is the difference between list-sections and get-documentation?
faq.answer: list-sections shows all available documentation topics. get-documentation retrieves full content for specific topics you select.

faq.question: Do I need to install @sveltejs/mcp separately?
faq.answer: No, the skill uses npx to run the package on-demand without global installation.

faq.question: Can this skill analyze TypeScript files?
faq.answer: Yes, it analyzes .svelte, .svelte.ts, and .svelte.js files with full TypeScript support.

faq.question: What does the autofixer actually fix?
faq.answer: It detects issues and provides suggestions. You must manually apply the recommended changes.

faq.question: Is this skill compatible with Svelte 4 projects?
faq.answer: Yes, use the --svelte-version 4 flag when running the autofixer for Svelte 4 code.

faq.question: Why do I need to escape $ in inline code?
faq.answer: The shell interprets $ as variable substitution. Escaping as \$ preserves the literal rune syntax.
